1.title: The former treatise
1.text: Luke calls his gospel the "former treatise" and addresses Acts to the same Theophilus. The two books form one work: first what Jesus did and taught, then what the church did in his name.

1.text: The mount called Olivet lay "a sabbath day's journey" from Jerusalem — roughly a thousand metres, the distance Jewish tradition allowed a person to walk on the day of rest.

2.text: Pentecost was a Jewish feast held fifty days after Passover. On this day the disciples, gathered with one accord in one place, received the Holy Ghost.

3.title: The gate called Beautiful
3.text: The lame man was laid daily at the temple gate called Beautiful to ask alms. It was a busy entrance, an ideal spot for someone who depended on the charity of those going up to pray.

4.text: The Sadducees denied any resurrection of the dead. That is why they were so disturbed to find the apostles preaching that Jesus had risen, right inside the temple they oversaw.

5.title: Ananias and Sapphira
5.text: The couple sold a possession and pretended to give the whole price, secretly keeping part back. Luke sets them against Barnabas in the previous chapter, who laid all he had at the apostles' feet.

To ask alms of the apostles

5.title: The Sadducees
5.text: The high priest and his company were of the sect of the Sadducees, who denied the resurrection. That is why the apostles' preaching of the risen Christ filled them with indignation.

5.title: Gamaliel
5.text: Gamaliel was a Pharisee and a doctor of the law, had in reputation among all the people. His measured counsel spared the apostles' lives: if the work were of God, no one could overthrow it.

That they were released without punishment

6.title: The seven chosen
6.text: The church was growing and the Greek-speaking widows were being overlooked in the daily distribution. The apostles did not abandon preaching: they delegated the material care to seven men of good report, often remembered as the first deacons.

6.question: What did the apostles decide not to abandon in order to serve tables?
options: The word of God and prayer

6.question: Why could Stephen's opponents not prevail against him in debate?
options: They could not resist the wisdom and the spirit by which he spoke

6.title: False witnesses
6.text: The charges against Stephen — destroying the temple and changing the customs of Moses — echo those made against Jesus himself at his trial. Luke shows the disciple following the steps of the Master.

pairs.right: Full of faith; his face seemed that of an angel
Chosen to serve tables

7.title: Stephen's speech
7.text: In his defense, Stephen walks through all of Israel's history — Abraham, Joseph, Moses, the tabernacle and the temple — not to justify himself, but to reveal a pattern: the people repeatedly rejected those whom God sent.

7.question: To whom did the God of glory appear, telling him to leave his country?
How many patriarchs did Jacob beget, according to Stephen's speech?

7.title: The burning bush
7.text: Stephen recalls Moses' call at Mount Sinai, in the flame of a bush that burned without being consumed. There God revealed himself as the God of Abraham, Isaac and Jacob.

7.template: the place where thou standest is {0} ground.

7.question: What did Israel do in the wilderness while rejecting Moses?
options: Made a calf and offered sacrifice to the idol

7.template: Heaven is my {0}, and earth is my footstool

7.question: What did Stephen declare he saw as he looked up to heaven?

An angel with a sword of fire

7.title: The first martyr
7.text: Stephen is the first Christian to die for the faith. As he is stoned, he prays for his killers — like Jesus on the cross — and Luke says he fell asleep. Among those present was Saul, who would soon become a persecutor.

8.title: The scattering
8.text: The persecution following Stephen's death scatters the disciples through Judea and Samaria. What looked like a blow against the church ends up spreading the gospel beyond Jerusalem.

8.question: What was Saul doing to the church before his conversion?
options: He made havoc of it, imprisoning men and women

8.template: down to the city of {0}, and preached Christ

8.title: Simon the sorcerer
8.text: Simon had bewitched Samaria with sorcery, so that the people called him the great power of God. Faced with Philip's miracles, he himself believed and was baptized.

8.question: How had Simon won the attention of the people of Samaria?
Why were Peter and John sent from Jerusalem to Samaria?
options: So that the believers might receive the Holy Ghost

8.title: Simony
8.text: When Simon offered money to buy the power of giving the Holy Ghost, Peter rebuked him sharply. From this episode comes the word simony: the sin of trying to buy or sell sacred things.

8.template: the {0} of God may be purchased with money.

8.question: What was the Ethiopian eunuch doing when Philip found him?
options: Reading the prophet Isaiah in his chariot

8.title: The lamb of Isaiah 53
8.text: The passage the eunuch was reading is from Isaiah 53, the song of the suffering servant led as a sheep to the slaughter. Philip began from that text to preach Jesus to him.

9.question: What did the voice from heaven say to Saul on the road to Damascus?
options: I am Jesus, whom thou persecutest

9.title: The Way
9.text: Saul carried letters to arrest those of the Way, one of the earliest names for Christianity. The light that surrounded him near Damascus would turn the greatest persecutor into the church's greatest missionary.

9.question: How long was Saul without sight after the vision?

9.title: Scales from the eyes
9.text: Ananias, a disciple afraid because of Saul's reputation, obeys God and lays hands on him. Something like scales falls from his eyes, and the persecutor begins to preach in the synagogues that Jesus is the Son of God.

9.title: Tabitha, the gazelle
9.text: Tabitha, in Greek Dorcas, means gazelle. A disciple in Joppa full of good works, she was loved by the widows she clothed. Peter raises her, and the news leads many to believe in the Lord.

9.question: Who was Tabitha (Dorcas), of Joppa?

A prophetess who foretold the future
The wife of Simon the tanner

10.title: Cornelius the centurion
10.text: Cornelius was a Roman officer who feared God, generous and given to prayer, yet not a Jew. His story opens the door of the gospel to the Gentiles, without their first having to adopt the law of Moses.

10.question: What did the angel tell Cornelius about his prayers and alms?

They were known only to men

10.question: What did Peter see descending from heaven in his vision?
options: A great sheet with all kinds of animals

That he should fast three days
That he should go up to the temple

10.title: Stand up, I am a man
10.text: When Cornelius falls at his feet, Peter at once lifts him up: I myself also am a man. Worship belongs to God alone, not to his messengers.

10.question: What did God show Peter through the vision of the sheet?
options: That he should call no man common or unclean
That he should rebuild the temple

That he should fast forty days

options: To hear all that God had commanded Peter

10.title: The Gentiles' Pentecost
10.text: While Peter was still speaking, the Holy Ghost fell on the Gentiles, who began to speak in tongues. The sign convinces Peter to baptize them, confirming that the gospel is for all peoples.

10.question: What astonished the believers of the circumcision who came with Peter?

An angel appeared in the house

11.title: The Gentiles and circumcision
11.text: The news that Gentiles had received the word of God stirred controversy among Jewish Christians, for whom eating with the uncircumcised broke the law. Peter has to explain the vision he had received in Joppa.

11.question: Why did the brethren of the circumcision criticize Peter?
options: For going in and eating with uncircumcised Gentiles

11.title: Called Christians
11.text: It was in Antioch, after a year of teaching by Barnabas and Saul, that the disciples were first called Christians. The city would become the base of Paul's missionary journeys.

12.title: Herod Agrippa I
12.text: The king of this chapter is Herod Agrippa I, grandson of Herod the Great. To please the Jews, he persecuted the church, killing James and imprisoning Peter.

12.question: Which apostle did Herod have killed with the sword?

12.title: The house of Mary
12.text: Freed from prison, Peter goes to the house of Mary, mother of John Mark, where the church was praying. John Mark would later be a traveling companion and, by tradition, the author of the Gospel of Mark.

12.question: Who recognized Peter's voice at the door but, for gladness, did not open it?
What did those gathered say when Rhoda insisted that Peter was at the door?

12.title: The death of Herod
12.text: Hailed by the people as the voice of a god, Herod gave no glory to God and was struck by an angel, dying eaten by worms. The historian Josephus records a similar death of the king at Caesarea.

12.template: he was eaten of {0}, and gave up the ghost.

13.title: The first missionary journey
13.text: As the church of Antioch fasted and prayed, the Holy Ghost set apart Barnabas and Saul for the work. Here begins the first of the missionary journeys that would carry the gospel across the Roman world.

13.question: Whom did the Holy Ghost command to set apart for the missionary work at Antioch?
Who was Elymas, who withstood Paul before the deputy in Cyprus?

13.title: From Saul to Paul
13.text: From this chapter on, Luke begins to call him Paul, his Roman name, instead of Saul, the Hebrew one. The change accompanies his growing leadership in the mission to the Gentiles.

14.title: Iconium, Lystra and Derbe
14.text: Paul and Barnabas keep preaching in cities of Asia Minor, always beginning at the synagogue. The response splits between faith and hostility, setting the pattern of the missionary journeys.

14.question: How was the city of Iconium divided by the apostles' preaching?
options: Divided: some with the Jews, some with the apostles

14.template: impotent in his {0}, being a cripple

14.title: The cripple of Lystra
14.text: At Lystra, Paul heals a man lame from birth who had never walked. The miracle echoes Peter's healing of the lame man at the temple early in Acts, showing the same power working through both apostles.

14.title: Jupiter and Mercury
14.text: Seeing the miracle, the people of Lystra took Barnabas for Jupiter and Paul for Mercury, the messenger of the gods, because he was the chief speaker. A local legend told that these gods had once visited the region in disguise.

14.question: What names did the crowd of Lystra give Barnabas and Paul, thinking them gods?
What happened to Paul at Lystra after Jews came from Antioch and Iconium?
options: He was stoned and dragged out as dead

14.question: What did Paul and Barnabas report to the church on returning to Antioch?
options: That God had opened the door of faith to the Gentiles
That they had been imprisoned in Rome
That the temple had been destroyed

pairs.right: Where Paul healed a cripple and was stoned
Names the crowd gave Barnabas and Paul
Ordained by Paul and Barnabas in every church
Opened by God to the Gentiles

15.title: The council of Jerusalem
15.text: The question was decisive: did Gentile converts need to be circumcised and keep the law of Moses? To settle it, the church held its first great council in Jerusalem.

15.question: What question divided the church and led to the council of Jerusalem?
options: Whether Gentiles needed to be circumcised to be saved
Which day to keep the sabbath

15.title: The yoke of the law
15.text: Peter called the demand for circumcision a yoke that neither their fathers nor they had been able to bear. His argument was that both Jews and Gentiles are saved by the grace of the Lord Jesus.

15.question: Who, after Peter, spoke up and quoted the prophets at the council?
Who were chosen to carry the council's letter to Antioch, with Paul and Barnabas?
What did the Holy Ghost and the apostles decide to lay upon the Gentiles?
options: No burden beyond a few necessary things

15.question: What did Paul and Barnabas do while they remained in Antioch?
options: They taught and preached the word of the Lord

15.question: Why did Paul and Barnabas part before the second journey?
options: They disagreed over taking John Mark with them

pairs.right: Decided Gentiles need not be circumcised
Proposed the final decision, quoting the prophets
Carried the council's letter to Antioch
Parted over John Mark

16.title: Timothy
16.text: Timothy, son of a Jewish mother and a Greek father, joins Paul at Lystra and would become one of his closest disciples. Paul would later write him two letters, 1 and 2 Timothy.

16.question: Who joined Paul at Lystra, the son of a Jewish mother and a Greek father?

16.template: Come over into {0}, and help us.

16.question: Who was Lydia, the first convert in Philippi?
options: A seller of purple from Thyatira who worshipped God
A slave girl with a spirit of divination

16.title: The spirit of divination
16.text: The slave girl who followed Paul had a spirit of divination that brought her masters much gain. When Paul cast it out in the name of Jesus, the source of income dried up — and that is why the masters dragged him to court.

16.question: What did Paul do to the girl who had a spirit of divination?
options: Cast out the spirit in the name of Jesus Christ
Sent her out of the city

16.template: And at {0} Paul and Silas prayed

16.title: The first church in Europe
16.text: The earthquake that opened the prison converted the jailer and his household. With Lydia, the jailer and the brethren, the first Christian community on the European continent forms in Philippi, to whom Paul would later send the letter to the Philippians.

16.question: What did the jailer ask Paul and Silas after the earthquake?
options: What must I do to be saved?

16.template: and was {0}, he and all his, straightway.

16.question: Why did Paul refuse to leave the prison secretly?
options: Because they were Roman citizens beaten without trial
Because the earthquake had destroyed the exit
Because they were waiting for Lydia

Seller of purple, first convert in Philippi

That the temple would be rebuilt

17.title: The noble Bereans
17.text: Luke praises the Jews of Berea for receiving the word with eagerness and searching the scriptures daily to check whether things were so. They became a model of those who study before believing.

17.template: searched the {0} daily, whether those things were so.

17.question: Which philosophers disputed with Paul in Athens?

17.title: To the Unknown God
17.text: In Athens, Paul found an altar dedicated “to the Unknown God” and used it as a bridge: the one the Athenians worshipped without knowing, he said, is the God who made heaven and earth. He even quoted Greek poets to proclaim him.

17.template: with this inscription, To the {0} God.
For in him we {0}, and move, and have our being;

17.question: What proof did God give that he will judge the world by the man he ordained?
options: He raised him from the dead

18.title: Aquila and Priscilla
18.text: In Corinth, Paul lodges with Aquila and Priscilla, a Jewish couple expelled from Rome by Claudius, and works with them making tents. The couple become constant co-workers of Paul and would help instruct Apollos.

18.question: Who believed on the Lord with all his house in Corinth?
options: Crispus, the chief ruler of the synagogue

18.question: What did the Lord say to Paul in a vision in Corinth?
options: Be not afraid, speak and hold not thy peace, for I have much people in this city
Preach no more to the Gentiles

18.question: How did the proconsul Gallio react to the Jews' accusation against Paul?
options: He refused to judge questions of words and names of the Jewish law

18.template: in Cenchrea: for he had a {0}.

18.title: Apollos of Alexandria
18.text: Apollos was a Jew eloquent and mighty in the scriptures, but he knew only the baptism of John. Priscilla and Aquila took him aside and explained the way of God to him more accurately, and he became a great preacher.

18.question: What did Priscilla and Aquila do on hearing Apollos in the synagogue of Ephesus?
options: Took him aside and explained the way of God more fully

pairs.right: Couple who made tents and instructed Apollos
Proconsul who refused to judge Paul
Synagogue ruler who believed in Corinth
Eloquent Jew of Alexandria, mighty in the scriptures

19.title: Ephesus and the temple of Diana
19.text: Ephesus was one of the empire's greatest cities and home to the temple of Diana (Artemis), one of the seven wonders of the ancient world. Paul would spend about three years there, his longest stay in a single city.

19.question: What did the disciples at Ephesus answer when Paul asked if they had received the Holy Ghost?
options: That they had not even heard whether there was a Holy Ghost
That they already spoke in tongues
That John had baptized them in the Spirit
That they did not believe in Jesus

19.question: How did God work extraordinary miracles through Paul in Ephesus?
options: Even handkerchiefs and aprons he had touched healed the sick

19.title: The sons of Sceva
19.text: Seven sons of Sceva tried to cast out an evil spirit by invoking “Jesus whom Paul preaches,” without faith of their own. The spirit answered that it knew Jesus and Paul, but not them, and the possessed man wounded them and made them flee naked.

19.template: Jesus I know, and {0} I know;
brought their {0} together, and burned them

19.title: Fifty thousand pieces of silver
19.text: The Ephesians who practiced magic burned their books in public; the value came to fifty thousand pieces of silver, a fortune. The act showed a radical and costly break with the old life.

19.question: Why did the silversmith Demetrius stir up a riot against Paul in Ephesus?
options: Because the preaching threatened his profit from Diana's silver shrines
Because Paul had destroyed the temple
Because Paul did not pay taxes

19.question: How did the townclerk describe Ephesus as he calmed the crowd?
options: As guardian of the temple of the great goddess Diana
As the holy city of the Jews

19.question: What warning did the townclerk give the crowd of Ephesus?
options: That they risked being charged with sedition for that uproar
That the temple would be closed

pairs.right: Rebaptized in the name of the Lord Jesus
Exorcists overcome by the evil spirit
Silversmith who stirred the riot for Diana
Calmed the crowd in the theatre

20.title: Eutychus and the first day of the week
20.text: In Troas, the disciples gathered on the first day of the week to break bread — one of the earliest mentions of Christian Sunday worship. It was during Paul's long sermon that the young Eutychus fell asleep and dropped from the window.

20.question: From where did Paul and his companions sail after the unleavened bread, toward Troas?
What happened to the young Eutychus during Paul's long sermon in Troas?
options: He fell asleep, dropped from the third loft and was taken up dead
He was arrested by the guards

20.template: to be at Jerusalem the day of {0}.

20.question: How did Paul describe his ministry to the elders of Ephesus?
options: He kept back nothing profitable, teaching publicly and house to house
He preached only in the temple

20.template: saying that {0} and afflictions abide me.

20.title: Shepherds of the flock
20.text: Paul charges the elders of Ephesus to watch over the flock as overseers, whom the Holy Ghost has appointed. He warns that “grievous wolves” and deceiving teachers will arise, even from among themselves.

20.template: shall grievous {0} enter in among you,

20.question: What did Paul say about silver, gold and clothing during his ministry?
options: That he coveted none, supporting himself with his own hands
That he received much from the rich
That he gave them to the poor
That he kept them for the journey

20.template: It is more blessed to {0} than to receive.

20.title: The farewell at Miletus
20.text: On learning they would see Paul's face no more, the elders of Ephesus wept and fell on his neck. It is one of the most moving scenes in Acts, closing the apostle's farewell speech.

pairs.right: Young man who fell from the window and was revived by Paul
Met Paul at Miletus for the farewell
False teachers Paul foresaw arising
A saying of Jesus quoted by Paul

21.question: What did the disciples of Tyre tell Paul through the Spirit?
options: That he should not go up to Jerusalem
That he should preach to the Gentiles
That he should return to Antioch

21.title: Philip the evangelist
21.text: In Caesarea, Paul lodges with Philip the evangelist, one of the seven chosen in Acts 6 and the same who baptized the Ethiopian eunuch. His four daughters prophesied, a sign of the Spirit's presence in the home.

21.question: How did Paul answer those who wept, begging him not to go up to Jerusalem?
options: That he was ready to die at Jerusalem for the name of the Lord Jesus
That he would change his route
That he would return to Caesarea
That he would wait for a sign

21.question: What did the brethren of Jerusalem tell Paul about the thousands of Jews who believed?
options: That they were all zealous of the law
That they wanted to kill him
That they did not believe in the resurrection

21.template: four men which have a {0} on them;

21.question: What did the Jews of Asia accuse Paul of as they stirred up the people in the temple?
options: Of teaching against the people, the law and the temple, and profaning it

21.title: The Court of the Gentiles
21.text: Gentiles could enter only the outer court of the temple; stone signs threatened death to anyone who crossed the barrier into the inner court. That is why the suspicion that Paul had taken the Greek Trophimus inside enraged the crowd.

21.question: What did the Roman commander do when he arrived and found Paul being beaten?
options: He arrested him and ordered him bound with two chains
He handed him to the Jews

21.question: For whom did the commander at first mistake Paul?
options: For an Egyptian who had led a revolt in the wilderness

pairs.right: Prophet who foretold Paul's arrest in Jerusalem
Hosted Paul in Caesarea; had four prophesying daughters
Ephesian Paul was accused of bringing into the temple
Roman officer who arrested Paul in the riot

22.title: At the feet of Gamaliel
22.text: Paul stresses that he was brought up in Jerusalem at the feet of Gamaliel, the respected Pharisee teacher who had earlier counseled caution against the apostles (Acts 5). His rigorous training explains the zeal with which he once persecuted Christians.

22.question: What did the voice say to Paul on the road to Damascus, in his defense?
options: I am Jesus of Nazareth, whom thou persecutest
I am the God of Abraham
I am an angel of the Lord

22.template: wash away thy {0}, calling on the name of the Lord.

22.question: To whom did Jesus tell Paul, in the temple, that he would send him?

22.template: to scourge a man that is a {0}, and uncondemned?

22.title: A citizen by birth
22.text: The commander had bought Roman citizenship with a great sum; Paul, however, was born a citizen, a rare and valuable status in a province. That right protected him from being scourged without trial and would shape his whole case.

22.question: What difference was there between Paul's Roman citizenship and the commander's?
options: Paul was born a citizen; the commander bought it for a great sum
Paul bought it and the commander inherited it

pairs.right: Teacher of the law at whose feet Paul was raised
Restored Saul's sight and told him to be baptized
Protected Paul from being scourged without trial
Martyr in whose death Paul had consented

23.question: What did Paul call the high priest Ananias after being struck on the mouth?

23.template: I am a {0}, the son of a Pharisee:

23.title: Sadducees and Pharisees
23.text: Paul divided the council by appealing to the resurrection: the Sadducees denied the resurrection, angels and spirits, while the Pharisees believed in all. The move turned the trial into an internal dispute of the Sanhedrin.

23.question: What did the Lord say to Paul the night after the uproar?
options: That as he had testified in Jerusalem, he must also testify in Rome
That he should flee to Damascus
That he should keep silent before Caesar
That he should return to Antioch

23.question: Who revealed to the commander the plot to kill Paul?
options: The son of Paul's sister (his nephew)

23.question: Where did the commander send Paul under heavy escort for safety?

23.template: unto the most excellent governor {0} sendeth greeting.

23.question: What did Felix decide on receiving Paul in Caesarea?
options: To hear him when his accusers came, keeping him in Herod's hall
To hand him to the Jews

pairs.right: High priest who ordered Paul struck on the mouth
Denied the resurrection, angels and spirits
Warned the commander of the plot
Commander who sent Paul to Felix

24.title: Tertullus the orator
24.text: The Jews hired Tertullus, a professional orator, to present the accusation against Paul before Felix in legal style. He opened by flattering the governor, a common technique in Roman courts.

24.question: Whom did the Jews bring to present the charges against Paul before Felix?

24.template: a ringleader of the sect of the {0}:

24.question: What did Paul confess about 'the Way' they called a sect?
options: That by it he served the God of the fathers, believing the law and the prophets
That it was a new religion

24.template: I came to bring {0} to my nation, and offerings.

24.question: Why did Felix tremble on hearing Paul speak of righteousness and the judgment to come?
options: He was seized with fear and put off the conversation

24.title: Two years in prison
24.text: Felix hoped Paul would give him money for his release, and so sent for him often. On leaving office, to please the Jews, he left Paul bound, handing the case to his successor Porcius Festus.

pairs.right: Orator who accused Paul before Felix
Governor who deferred the case and kept Paul bound
Jewish wife of Felix
Felix's successor as governor

25.title: Appeal to Caesar
25.text: Appealing to Caesar was a Roman citizen's right that took the case out of local hands to the emperor's court in Rome. By using it, Paul unknowingly fulfilled the Lord's promise that he would testify in Rome.

25.question: What did the chief of the Jews ask Festus concerning Paul?
options: That he bring him to Jerusalem, plotting to kill him on the way
That he send him to Rome

25.template: neither against the temple, nor yet against {0}, have I offended

25.question: What decision did Paul make when pressured to go to Jerusalem?
options: He agreed to be judged by the Jews

25.question: What was the heart of the Jews' dispute against Paul, according to Festus?
options: Questions of their religion and of one Jesus, dead, whom Paul affirmed to be alive

25.question: What did king Agrippa say to Festus about Paul?
options: That he also wished to hear the man himself
That he condemn him to death
That he send him to Jerusalem

25.template: he himself hath appealed to {0}, I have determined to send him.

pairs.right: New governor who succeeded Felix
Came to Caesarea and heard Paul's case
Roman citizen's right that Paul used
The emperor to whom Paul appealed

26.title: Before king Agrippa
26.text: Agrippa II was well versed in the customs and questions of the Jews, which makes this Paul's fullest defense in Acts. It is the third account of his conversion in the book, now before a king.

26.question: According to the strictest sect of his religion, how did Paul say he had lived?

26.template: many of the {0} did I shut up in prison,

26.question: In what language did the voice from heaven speak to Paul on the road to Damascus?

26.template: to turn them from darkness to {0},

26.question: How did Festus react to Paul's speech before Agrippa?
options: He said aloud that much learning was making him mad

26.title: Almost thou persuadest me
26.text: Agrippa's famous reply — 'almost thou persuadest me to be a Christian' — shows how deeply Paul's defense touched the king. Paul replied wishing that all his hearers might become as he was, except for the chains.

26.question: What did king Agrippa reply to Paul?
options: Almost he was persuaded to become a Christian
That he condemned him to death
That he would free him at once

pairs.right: Heard Paul's defense and was almost persuaded to be a Christian
Said much learning was making Paul mad
Paul's call to be a minister and witness
Prevented Paul from being released

27.title: The voyage to Rome
27.text: The account returns to the first person plural ('we'), a sign that Luke was traveling with Paul. As a prisoner who had appealed to Caesar, Paul is sent to Rome under the guard of the centurion Julius, of the Augustan band.

27.question: Where were Paul and other prisoners sent, under the guard of the centurion Julius?

27.title: The grain ships
27.text: At Myra, the centurion puts the group on a ship of Alexandria bound for Italy. These were large freighters carrying grain from Egypt to feed Rome — hence the wheat later thrown overboard to lighten the ship.

27.question: What did Paul warn about the sea voyage?
options: That it would bring hurt and loss, to the ship, the cargo and their lives
That the wind would be favorable
That they should sell the cargo

27.question: Why did the majority decide to leave Fair Havens and try to reach Phenice?
options: Because the haven was not suitable to winter in

27.question: What did the crew do when violently tossed by the storm?
options: Lightened the ship, casting cargo and tackle into the sea

27.title: No life will be lost
27.text: At the height of the storm, when all had lost hope, an angel assured Paul that he would stand before Caesar and that no one aboard would die. Paul then encouraged the 276 to eat and to trust God.

27.question: What did the sailors suspect at midnight on the fourteenth night of the storm?
options: That they were nearing some land
That the ship would sink at once

27.template: there shall not an {0} fall from the head of any of you.

27.question: How many people were on the ship in all?
Why did the centurion stop the soldiers from killing the prisoners in the wreck?
options: Because he wanted to save Paul

pairs.right: Centurion of the Augustan band who guarded Paul
Tempestuous wind that drove the ship
Macedonian of Thessalonica who accompanied Paul
All escaped safe to land after the wreck

28.title: From murderer to god
28.text: The people of Malta, seeing the viper fastened on Paul's hand, first judged him a murderer punished by divine justice; seeing that nothing happened to him, they began to call him a god. Paul simply shook the snake into the fire, suffering no harm.

28.template: there came a {0} out of the heat, and fastened on his hand.

28.question: What did Paul do for the father of Publius, sick with fever and dysentery?
options: Prayed, laid his hands on him, and healed him

28.question: How did Paul react on being met by the brethren at Appii Forum and the Three Taverns?
options: He thanked God and took courage

28.template: for the {0} of Israel I am bound with this chain.

28.question: How did Paul spend the day with the Jews in Rome?
options: Declaring the kingdom of God and persuading them about Jesus from the law and the prophets

28.title: The open ending of Acts
28.text: Acts ends without narrating Paul's final trial: Luke's focus is the gospel reaching Rome freely, capital of the world. The Isaiah quote about the hardened heart seals the decisive turn of the message to the Gentiles.

28.template: with all {0}, no man forbidding him.

pairs.right: Island where Paul survived a viper's bite
Chief of the island whose father Paul healed
Where Paul preached for two years as a prisoner, yet freely
Prophet Paul quoted about the hardened heart
